student_message: Oh cool! The arms match side-to-side, and top-to-bottom they match the legs, so 'X' has two fold lines! What if we fold it diagonally like a railroad crossing sign, does that count as a fold line too?
final_message:
You have amazing math eyes, Zara! ✨ Yes, folding it diagonally absolutely counts as a fold line. 

If you have a perfectly shaped letter $$X$$ and you fold it right down one of its slanted lines, the other two arms will fold over and match perfectly like a mirror image. Since there are two crisscrossing slanted lines, that gives us two diagonal fold lines. When we add those to the up-and-down and side-to-side lines you already found, a perfect letter $$X$$ actually has four lines of symmetry! 

You are doing such a wonderful job finding all these special mirror lines. Can you think of any shapes that might also have diagonal fold lines?

orchestrator_intent.goal: Explain diagonal lines of symmetry for the letter X.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student is happy, focused, and asking a great conceptual question about diagonal lines of symmetry. The dialogue agent should answer her question to deepen her understanding.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
session_summary: Zara covered rotational ("spinning") and reflectional ("folding") symmetry. She understood the concepts exceptionally well, correctly identifying that letters like 'Z' have only rotational symmetry, while squares and plus signs possess both. Zara exhibited no struggles during this session. Her current learning state is highly engaged and progressive; having firmly grasped 2D symmetry rules, she is now proactively inquiring about how symmetry applies to 3D objects like basketballs.
